raw_case:
COL4A1 Gene Mutation Masquerading as Cerebral Palsy: Report of a Rare Case
The Collagen Type 4 alpha 1 (COL4A1), is an important component of nearly all vascular basement membranes. Pathogenic mutation of this gene results in varied manifestations. In this report, we describe a two-and-a-half-year-old boy with an eventful perinatal period, global developmental delay, and epileptic spasms. Examination revealed microcephaly, nystagmus, and spasticity in limbs. Electroencephalogram showed multifocal epileptiform discharges and MRI brain demonstrated periventricular white matter changes, intracerebral bleeds, and porencephalic cysts. CT brain showed intracranial calcifications and screening for congenital infection was negative. The molecular genetic evaluation was later confirmed with a heterozygous mutation of the COL4A1 gene on exon 37 (variant - p.Gly1050Ala) with an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Currently, the child has developed drug-refractory epilepsy requiring polypharmacy and the ketogenic diet. COL4A1 gene mutations are close mimickers of Cerebral Palsy, hence a high index of suspicion should be exercised while approaching a child with spastic quadriplegia in order to promptly diagnose and manage such children for a better neurological outcome. 

Introduction

The Collagen alpha-1(IV)(COL4A)-related disorder spectrum is an inherited genetic disorder, comprising two genes - COL4A1 and COL4A2. Both of these genes are located on chromosome number 13 and are inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, with a small proportion (27%) of cases attributed to a de novo pathogenic variant [1]. These genes form the major structural component of type IV collagen, which in turn helps in cell migration, proliferation, cell differentiation, and survival. It was first described by Gould et al. in 2005 as a cause of perinatal cerebral hemorrhage in mice, however, in humans, it can present as a wide range of phenotypes [2]. Mutations in these genes manifest as weakening of basement vascular membranes thereby causing hemorrhage, small vessel diseases, familial porencephaly, and the HANAC (hereditary angiopathy with nephropathy, aneurysms, and muscle cramps) syndrome. A total of 67 families with COL4A1 and COL4A2 mutations have been described in the literature [1]. Clinically, small-vessel brain disease can present with a range of neurological symptoms. These may include infantile hemiparesis, seizures, single or recurrent hemorrhagic strokes, ischemic strokes, and isolated migraines with aura. Apart from this, two additional phenotypes include isolated retinal artery tortuosity and congenital cataracts. Here, we describe a two-and-a-half-year-old boy with global developmental delay (GDD) and epileptic encephalopathy secondary to a genetically proven COL4A1 mutation.

Case presentation

A two-and-a-half-year-old boy, with delayed attainment of milestones presented with epileptic spasms since one year of age. He was the firstborn out of a non-consanguineous marriage. He had an eventful perinatal period with a history of pregnancy-induced hypertension in the mother. He was born prematurely (at 35 weeks of gestation) with a birth weight of 1.7 kg, requiring admission to the neonatal intensive care unit in view of respiratory distress for 7 days.All the milestones were attained late with a predominant motor developmental delay as opposed to cognitive delay. Since one year of age, the child had an onset of epileptic spasms. There was no significant family history. The child's anthropometric measures were appropriate for age. Examination revealed no obvious dysmorphic features and no neuro-cutaneous markers, however, the child had microcephaly (head circumference (HC) - 42 cm), nystagmus, and squint. Spasticity was present in all four limbs with exaggerated deep tendon reflexes. Other systemic examinations revealed no abnormality. The ophthalmologic evaluation revealed no cataracts or pigmentary retinopathy.Electroencephalogram showed multifocal epileptiform discharges with intermittent bilaterally synchronous generalization and absence of sleep architecture (Figure 1). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) Brain showed periventricular hyperintensities, periventricular cysts, ex vacuo dilatation of lateral ventricles with squaring of occipital horns, white matter volume loss, and evidence of old intraventricular hemorrhages (Figure 2). So we went ahead with computed tomography (CT) brain to look for calcifications. It revealed multiple calcifications in bilateral periventricular white matter and basal ganglia (Figure 3).Since the clinical presentation was like a static encephalopathy with perinatal events and MRI findings suggestive of sequelae of preterm brain injury, an initial differential diagnosis of spastic quadriplegia was considered. The presence of intracranial calcification hinted towards possibilities of congenital infections as well as genetic causes like Aicardi Gautier's syndrome and COL4A1 mutations. Screening for congenital toxoplasmosis, rubella, cytomegalovirus (CMV), herpes simplex and human immunodeficiency viruses (TORCH) group of infections showed IgG CMV positivity which was nonspecific, and a negative urine CMV-PCR test. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was done to look for renal cysts, which revealed no abnormality and echocardiography also revealed a structurally normal heart. The child was started on anti-seizure medications with hormonal therapy for infantile spasms and neurorehabilitation was initiated. His spasms were drug-resistant, requiring polypharmacy (levitiracetam, valproate, clobazam, vigabatrin) and initiation of a ketogenic diet.Subsequent whole genome sequencing revealed a heterozygous mutation in the COL4A1 gene on exon 37 of chromosome 13 with an autosomal dominant inheritance. This was classified as a variant of uncertain significance. The clinical phenotype as well as the neuroimaging findings matched with the genotype even though it was a variant of unknown significance. MRI Brain and Sanger sequencing were recommended to the parents, after having been screened for renal cysts and cataracts. 

Discussion

COL4A1 mutations were initially described in humans as autosomal dominant porencephaly [2]. The phenotype not only involves the brain but affects the eyes and kidneys as well. The eye manifestations include cataracts, retinal artery tortuosities, and Axenfeld Rieger anomaly. The renal manifestations include renal cysts, hematuria, and renal failure [3]. About 67 families have been reported in the literature with a few cases reported from India as well [4]. However, many cases go underdiagnosed probably due to lack of testing, and are treated as non-genetic forms of static encephalopathy. Hence, more often than not COL4A1 mutations are known as ‘Cerebral Palsy’ mimics. The various neurological manifestations of this spectrum of disorder are usually secondary to intracerebral bleeds, porencephalic cysts, periventricular white matter involvement, and intraventricular hemorrhage [5,6].Intracerebral bleeds can happen at any age starting from fetal life through childhood and adulthood. These can occur spontaneously or after minor trauma. Neurological abnormalities can vary from microcephaly, spastic quadriplegia, and infantile hemiplegia. The child was started on anti-seizure medications with hormonal therapy for infantile spasms and neurorehabilitation was initiated. Our case presented with microcephaly and spastic quadriplegia without cataracts. Seizures are very commonly reported in COL4A1 spectrum disorders. Our patient presented with epileptic spasms which were drug refractory. Spasms have been reported in previous studies as well [5]. Epileptogenesis in patients with COL4A mutations can be explained by the development of cerebral vascular infarcts and microbleeds secondary to disrupted cerebral basement membranes, leading to increased neuronal excitotoxicity, changes in white matter, and impaired neuronal migration. This in turn leads to altered synapse formations, cortical malformations, gliotic changes, and white matter damage, thereby causing epilepsy, microcephaly, and developmental delays.The neuroimaging findings of COL4A1 spectrum disorders usually include microinfarcts in the centrum semiovale and deep gray matter. Because the microvasculature in the basal ganglia region has sparse supporting tissue, there are higher incidences of microhemorrhages in this region [5]. Perinatal brain hemorrhages in fetuses and newborns have been described as the commonest manifestation of COL4A1 mutation [2]. This may be due to endothelial basement membrane weakness. Along with this, porencephalic cysts are often noted. These cysts are usually unilateral [7]. Our case had intraventricular hemorrhages as well as porencephalic cysts and periventricular white matter gliosis. Calcification over basal ganglia as well as periventricular white matter was also seen in our case. Intracranial calcifications especially in basal ganglia have been previously reported in cases with COL4A1 mutation [8,9]. The other neuroimaging findings described in the literature are schizencephaly, cortical developmental malformations, focal cortical dysplasia, hydranencephaly, and hydrocephaly [1,10].The clinical and neuroimaging findings like periventricular leukoencephalopathy, intraventricular hemorrhage, and parenchymal bleeds very closely mimic preterm hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy sequelae and intraventricular hemorrhage, and the calcifications mimic intrauterine infections in a child with static encephalopathy. In India, the prevalence of cerebral palsy is high with a high burden of prematurity, perinatal events, and congenital infections. Hence, we must have a high index of suspicion to diagnose this ‘Cerebral Palsy Mimic’.

Conclusions

COL4A1 mutations are close mimickers of static encephalopathy with characteristic neuroimaging findings. The clinical clues, along with molecular markers of COL4A1, neuroimaging findings, and a high index of suspicion should prompt quick diagnosis and initiation of treatment for better neurological outcomes and quality of life in these patients as it will guide in genetic counseling.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: A 2.5-year-old male, firstborn of a non-consanguineous marriage.
- Chief Complaint: None explicitly stated; primary presentation includes developmental delay and epileptic spasms.
- History of Present Illness: Delayed motor milestones, onset of epileptic spasms at one year of age. No significant cognitive delay. Perinatal history notable for prematurity (35 weeks), pregnancy-induced hypertension in the mother, birth weight of 1.7 kg, and neonatal respiratory distress requiring NICU admission for 7 days.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: None specified.
- Family History: None significant.
- Physical Examination: Microcephaly (HC: 42 cm), nystagmus, squint, spasticity in all four limbs, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes. No dysmorphic features or neurocutaneous markers. Other systemic examinations were unremarkable.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Electroencephalogram: Multifocal epileptiform discharges, bilaterally synchronous generalization, absence of sleep architecture.
  - MRI Brain: Periventricular hyperintensities, porencephalic cysts, ex vacuo dilatation of lateral ventricles, white matter volume loss, evidence of old intraventricular hemorrhages.
  - CT Brain: Intracranial calcifications in periventricular white matter and basal ganglia.
  - Screening for congenital infections (TORCH): Negative except for nonspecific IgG CMV positivity; urine CMV-PCR negative.
  - Ultrasonography: No renal cysts.
  - Echocardiography: Structurally normal heart.
  - Genetic Testing: Whole genome sequencing revealed heterozygous mutation in COL4A1 gene on exon 37 (variant: p.Gly1050Ala, autosomal dominant inheritance).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1: Initial Considerations**  
  The presentation with spastic quadriplegia, delayed motor development, and intracranial abnormalities (hemorrhages, calcifications, porencephalic cysts) raised suspicion for static encephalopathy or cerebral palsy-like conditions. Potential causes included perinatal brain injury, congenital infections, or genetic disorders.

- **Step 2: Exclusion of Congenital Infections**  
  Intracranial calcifications prompted screening for congenital TORCH infections. Negative PCR and nonspecific CMV IgG ruled out active or significant perinatal infections.

- **Step 3: Consideration of Genetic Causes**  
  Periventricular white matter involvement, porencephalic cysts, and calcifications indicated genetic conditions like Aicardi-Goutières Syndrome or COL4A1 mutations. No specific features (e.g., retinal or renal findings) pointed to Aicardi-Goutières Syndrome.

- **Step 4: Confirmation of COL4A1 Mutation**  
  Genetic testing confirmed a heterozygous mutation in the COL4A1 gene (variant p.Gly1050Ala) with autosomal dominant inheritance. Findings matched the clinical phenotype and neuroimaging features, despite the variant being of "uncertain significance."

- **Step 5: Exclusion of Other Conditions**  
  No ophthalmologic abnormalities (e.g., cataracts, retinal artery tortuosities) or renal abnormalities (e.g., cysts) associated with COL4A1-related spectrum were present, narrowing the diagnosis to its neurological phenotype.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: COL4A1-related disorder presenting as a mimic of cerebral palsy with static encephalopathy, spastic quadriplegia, microcephaly, global developmental delay, drug-refractory seizures, and neuroimaging features of periventricular calcifications, white matter gliosis, porencephalic cysts, and old hemorrhages.

- Patient Information: A 2.5-year-old male, firstborn of a non-consanguineous marriage.
- Chief Complaint: None explicitly stated; primary presentation includes developmental delay and epileptic spasms.
- History of Present Illness: Delayed motor milestones, onset of epileptic spasms at one year of age. No significant cognitive delay. Perinatal history notable for prematurity (35 weeks), pregnancy-induced hypertension in the mother, birth weight of 1.7 kg, and neonatal respiratory distress requiring NICU admission for 7 days.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: None specified.
- Family History: None significant.
- Physical Examination: Microcephaly (HC: 42 cm), nystagmus, squint, spasticity in all four limbs, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes. No dysmorphic features or neurocutaneous markers. Other systemic examinations were unremarkable.

  - Electroencephalogram: Multifocal epileptiform discharges, bilaterally synchronous generalization, absence of sleep architecture.
  - MRI Brain: Periventricular hyperintensities, porencephalic cysts, ex vacuo dilatation of lateral ventricles, white matter volume loss, evidence of old intraventricular hemorrhages.

  - Screening for congenital infections (TORCH): Negative except for nonspecific IgG CMV positivity; urine CMV-PCR negative.

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's case. The child is 2.5 years old with developmental delay and epileptic spasms. The perinatal history includes prematurity at 35 weeks, maternal hypertension, low birth weight, and neonatal respiratory distress requiring NICU care. The physical exam shows microcephaly, nystagmus, squint, spasticity in all limbs, and exaggerated reflexes. The MRI and CT scans show periventricular hyperintensities, porencephalic cysts, white matter loss, old intraventricular hemorrhages, and calcifications in the white matter and basal ganglia. The genetic test found a heterozygous mutation in COL4A1, specifically p.Gly1050Ala, which is autosomal dominant.

First, I need to connect the clinical findings with the genetic mutation. COL4A1 is part of the collagen IV alpha-1 chain, which is crucial for basement membranes in the brain and other organs. Mutations in COL4A1 are known to cause various neurological conditions, including hereditary angiopathy with retinal and cerebral involvement (HARCI), but also other forms of cerebral small vessel disease. The presence of periventricular calcifications and white matter abnormalities on imaging is a key point here. 

The patient's MRI shows periventricular hyperintensities, porencephalic cysts, and white matter volume loss, which are consistent with brain malformations due to collagen IV defects. The CT showing calcifications in the periventricular white matter and basal ganglia further supports this. These calcifications are characteristic of some collagen IV disorders, especially when they affect the brain's vasculature.

The epileptic spasms and developmental delay are also important. Epileptic spasms in infants can be associated with various conditions, including tuberous sclerosis or other metabolic disorders, but the imaging findings here are more in line with a vascular or structural issue. The absence of dysmorphic features and neurocutaneous markers makes some other conditions less likely.

The genetic test result is a heterozygous mutation in COL4A1. While some mutations in COL4A1 are autosomal dominant, the specific variant p.Gly1050Ala might be pathogenic. It's important to check if this variant is known to cause disease. However, the presence of the mutation along with the imaging findings and clinical symptoms strongly points towards a diagnosis related to COL4A1 mutations.

The perinatal history of prematurity and intraventricular hemorrhage might be contributing factors, but the genetic mutation likely explains the underlying cause. The combination of microcephaly, spasticity, and the imaging findings with the genetic test result all align with a diagnosis of a COL4A1-related disorder. 

I should also consider other differential diagnoses, such as other inherited metabolic disorders or congenital infections, but the TORCH tests were negative except for CMV IgG, which is not specific here. The absence of renal cysts on ultrasound and normal echocardiography also rule out some other conditions associated with COL4A1 mutations, like Alport syndrome, which is more related to renal and auditory issues. 

Putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is a COL4A1 mutation causing a cerebral small vessel disease with the associated neurological symptoms and imaging findings.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's clinical presentation of developmental delay, epileptic spasms, microcephaly, spasticity, and nystagmus, combined with imaging findings of periventricular hyperintensities, porencephalic cysts, white matter volume loss, and intracranial calcifications, points toward a neurodevelopmental disorder with a structural brain abnormality. The perinatal history of prematurity, maternal hypertension, and neonatal respiratory distress may have contributed to cerebral injury, but the genetic finding of a COL4A1 mutation suggests an inherited etiology.  
<step 2> The COL4A1 gene encodes a component of collagen IV, critical for basement membrane integrity. Mutations in COL4A1 are associated with cerebral small vessel disease, periventricular calcifications, and white matter abnormalities, which align with the MRI and CT findings. Autosomal dominant inheritance of COL4A1 mutations can lead to conditions such as hereditary angiopathy with retinal and cerebral involvement (HARCI) or other collagen IV-related disorders.  
<step 3> The specific variant p.Gly1050Ala in COL4A1 is a heterozygous mutation, which is pathogenic in the context of the patient's clinical and radiological findings. The absence of renal or auditory abnormalities (e.g., Alport syndrome) and the presence of calcifications and porencephalic cysts further support a diagnosis linked to COL4A1 dysfunction rather than other collagenopathies.  
<step 4> The lack of dysmorphic features, neurocutaneous markers, or congenital infection evidence (except non-specific CMV IgG) rules out other syndromes. The combination of genetic, neuroimaging, and clinical data confirms a diagnosis of a COL4A1-related disorder, specifically a form of cerebral small vessel disease with periventricular calcifications and developmental complications.  

### Answer:
COL4A1-related cerebral small vessel disease with periventricular calcifications and developmental delay.  
```
